texto: The Infinite cannot be measured. The plan of Nature is so immense, but she has no plan, no scheme, but to go on and on forever. What is size, what is time, distance, to the Infinite? Nothing. The Infinite knows no time, no space, no great, no small, no beginning, no end.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de John Burroughs que provoca limites e escolhas conscientes. Ao questionar “The Infinite cannot be measured. The plan of Nature is so immense, but she has no plan, no scheme…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre tempo, nature, time, com foco em termino e time.

texto: The universe is so unhuman, that is, it goes its way with so little thought of man. He is but an incident, not an end. We must adjust our notions to the discovery that things are not shaped to him, but that he is shaped to them. The air was not made for his lungs, but he has lungs because there is air; the light was not created for his eye, but he has eyes because there is light. All the forces of nature are going their own way; man avails himself of them, or catches a ride as best he can. If he keeps his seat, he prospers; if he misses his hold and falls, he is crushed.
explicacao: Orientação direta de John Burroughs sobre temas ligados a autoestima. A afirmação “The universe is so unhuman, that is, it goes its way with so little thought of man. He is but an …” funciona como lembrete prático ligado a autoestima, evolution, nature, útil em momentos de termino e nature.

texto: Birds and animals probably think without knowing that they think; that is, they have not self-consciousness.
